title: Overflow — the width Python hides from you
problem: Count upward in a fixed 8-bit register (it can only hold 0 to 255) and see what happens past the top.

trace.note: Same start of 254 — but plain Python this time. Python's integers have no fixed width.

trace.checkpoint_prompt: count is at 255. After `count = count + 1` runs in plain Python (no fixed width), what will count be?

trace.note: count is 256 — no wrap. Python grows the integer as large as it needs; it would happily reach a thousand-digit number. The fixed-width overflow is hidden from you, which is exactly why it surprises people working close to real hardware.
258 and still climbing. Compare with the 8-bit register, which wrapped back to 0 three ticks ago. Same counting, completely different behaviour — because one has a fixed width and one does not.
